title: Manage a contract - Financial security | CanadaBuys

structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: Financial security

structure_blocks.heading: Financial security
structure_blocks.text: Consider the aspects described below when dealing with financial security.

structure_blocks.heading: Release of contract financial security
structure_blocks.text: Release of contract financial security

structure_blocks.heading: Release of contract financial security
structure_blocks.text: Surety bonds automatically expire and must be destroyed once the contractor has met all contractual obligations.

structure_blocks.heading: Release of contract financial security
structure_blocks.text: For contracts where security deposits like government-guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange or irrevocable standby letters of credit were provided, they must be returned if the contract ends without fault on the contractor's part.

structure_blocks.heading: Release of contract financial security
structure_blocks.text: You must direct the Finance Branch to either requisition a cheque for the amount of a bill of exchange including accumulated interest or arrange for the release of any bonds, letters of credit or other negotiable instruments deposited.

structure_blocks.heading: Issues related to amendments
structure_blocks.text: Issues related to amendments

structure_blocks.heading: Issues related to amendments
structure_blocks.text: Before approving significant material changes to contract conditions, buyers should ensure they don't invalidate security obligations. Consent from the surety company should be obtained for material changes such as adjustments to the contract price, scope of work, completion or delivery dates and payment schedules.

structure_blocks.heading: Issues related to amendments
structure_blocks.text: For amendments that introduce changes, buyers should send a draft amendment to the surety company for approval. If the contract allows for specific design or engineering changes within set limits, prior consent from the surety is not needed but the company should be informed. Consent is necessary if these limits are altered.

structure_blocks.heading: Adjusting security with contract price changes
heading_path: Adjusting security with contract price changes
structure_blocks.text: Adjusting security with contract price changes

structure_blocks.heading: Adjusting security with contract price changes
heading_path: Adjusting security with contract price changes
structure_blocks.text: When the contract price increases, consider raising the security amount to reflect the new risk. The amount on a contract support letter of credit can be adjusted up or down based on changes in risk.

structure_blocks.heading: Adjusting security with contract price changes
heading_path: Adjusting security with contract price changes
structure_blocks.text: The adjustment can be made through an amendment or as specified in the letter of credit for changes on set dates. An adjustment can also be made upon conditions like the presentation of an interim certificate of completion being met. Ensure any new letter of credit is in place before amending the contract.

structure_blocks.heading: Adjusting security with contract price changes
heading_path: Adjusting security with contract price changes
structure_blocks.text: If security deposits exceed the necessary amount due to contract price changes, the excess must be returned to the contractor. Similarly, you should reduce the face amount of the letter of credit to align with the decreased risk, following the procedure outlined for increasing the amount.

structure_blocks.heading: Difficulty fulfilling contract requirements
structure_blocks.text: Difficulty fulfilling contract requirements

structure_blocks.heading: Difficulty fulfilling contract requirements
structure_blocks.text: If a contractor shows signs of difficulty fulfilling the contract requirements and there is financial security in place, you should immediately notify the surety company. This step ensures proactive management of risks associated with contract fulfillment.

heading_path: Custody and safekeeping of financial security and handling of bills of exchange
structure_blocks.text: Bills of exchange used as security deposits for offers must be uncashed and securely stored until a successful offer is chosen, or for up to 1 year, whichever occurs first. If a contract has not been awarded by the year's end, the bill must be exchanged for one with a current date.

structure_blocks.heading: Custody and safekeeping of financial security and handling of bills of exchange
heading_path: Custody and safekeeping of financial security and handling of bills of exchange
structure_blocks.text: Bills of exchange and accompanying offer documentation are sent by the offer receiving unit to the Finance Sector of PSPC for secure holding. The Finance Sector confirms receipt in writing to the offer receiving unit. The receiving unit provides appropriate documentation to the buyer for the procurement file.

structure_blocks.text: Once an offer is accepted the bill of exchange is required as security until completion of the contract. If requested by the contractor, the bill of exchange should be retained uncashed and stored in secure approved facilities. If suitable storage is unavailable, the bill of exchange must be sent to the directorate responsible for financial operations for safekeeping.

Bills of exchange after offer acceptance
structure_blocks.text: If no such request is made, the bill should be deposited directly into the Consolidated Revenue Fund (CRF) in accordance with public money handling regulations.

heading_path: Custody and safekeeping of financial security and handling of bills of exchange
structure_blocks.text: Bills of exchange must be returned to the contractor if the offer is rejected or accepted. The bill is not needed as ongoing security.

structure_blocks.heading: Managing received securities
heading_path: Custody and safekeeping of financial security and handling of bills of exchange
structure_blocks.text: All bills of exchange received as part of contract security must be forwarded for deposit to the directorate responsible for financial operations in the CRF . This is in line with the Receipt and Deposit of Public Money Regulations .

structure_blocks.heading: Security for plans and specifications
heading_path: Custody and safekeeping of financial security and handling of bills of exchange
structure_blocks.text: Security for plans and specifications

structure_blocks.heading: Security for plans and specifications
heading_path: Custody and safekeeping of financial security and handling of bills of exchange
structure_blocks.text: Security deposits provided for the return of plans and specifications are forfeited if they are not returned in a timely and satisfactory condition. You must immediately inform the relevant manager in the directorate responsible for financial operations if a security deposit is forfeited.

structure_blocks.heading: Recording and safekeeping
structure_blocks.text: Recording and safekeeping

structure_blocks.heading: Recording and safekeeping
structure_blocks.text: The Finance Sector of PSPC is responsible for recording all received government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, and irrevocable standby letters of credit in the accounting records of PSPC and the Accounts of Canada. Directorates must notify the Finance Sector of all such receipts regardless of where they are held.

structure_blocks.heading: Safekeeping methods for negotiable instruments
structure_blocks.text: Safekeeping methods for negotiable instruments

structure_blocks.heading: Safekeeping methods for negotiable instruments
structure_blocks.text: Safekeeping of government bonds, negotiable instruments and letters of credit can be ensured through:

structure_blocks.heading: Safekeeping methods for negotiable instruments
structure_blocks.text: custody by the directorate for financial operations

structure_blocks.heading: Safekeeping methods for negotiable instruments
structure_blocks.text: secure storage by the directorate using approved security equipment, as per Government Contracts Regulations

structure_blocks.heading: Safekeeping methods for negotiable instruments
structure_blocks.text: use of the Security Deposit Division facilities

structure_blocks.heading: Additional security measures
structure_blocks.text: Additional security measures

structure_blocks.heading: Additional security measures
structure_blocks.text: You must consider the following additional security measures:

structure_blocks.heading: Additional security measures
structure_blocks.text: the adequacy of departmental security equipment should be assessed regularly with guidance available through the PSPC Security Equipment Catalogue

structure_blocks.heading: Additional security measures
structure_blocks.text: in the absence of proper security equipment, use a designated deposit form to send all deposits to the directorate responsible for financial operations

structure_blocks.heading: Additional security measures
structure_blocks.text: to minimize risk and ensure security, transmit bonds directly to the directorate from where the security is first received

structure_blocks.heading: Additional security measures
structure_blocks.text: registered and hypothecated bonds must be sent via registered mail

structure_blocks.heading: Additional security measures
structure_blocks.text: bearer bonds may be sent using secure transport methods

structure_blocks.heading: Additional security measures
structure_blocks.text: additional insurance should be considered for bearer bonds sent through the “money packet” system to cover values exceeding Canada Post’s standard indemnity limit of $100

structure_blocks.heading: Additional security measures
structure_blocks.text: the directorate responsible for financial operations also manages the clipping of coupons from bearer bonds and coordinates their redemption

raw_text:
Financial security

Consider the aspects described below when dealing with financial security.

Release of contract financial security

Surety bonds automatically expire and must be destroyed once the contractor has met all contractual obligations.

For contracts where security deposits like government-guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange or irrevocable standby letters of credit were provided, they must be returned if the contract ends without fault on the contractor's part.

You must direct the Finance Branch to either requisition a cheque for the amount of a bill of exchange including accumulated interest or arrange for the release of any bonds, letters of credit or other negotiable instruments deposited.

Issues related to amendments

Before approving significant material changes to contract conditions, buyers should ensure they don't invalidate security obligations. Consent from the surety company should be obtained for material changes such as adjustments to the contract price, scope of work, completion or delivery dates and payment schedules.

For amendments that introduce changes, buyers should send a draft amendment to the surety company for approval. If the contract allows for specific design or engineering changes within set limits, prior consent from the surety is not needed but the company should be informed. Consent is necessary if these limits are altered.

Adjusting security with contract price changes

When the contract price increases, consider raising the security amount to reflect the new risk. The amount on a contract support letter of credit can be adjusted up or down based on changes in risk.

The adjustment can be made through an amendment or as specified in the letter of credit for changes on set dates. An adjustment can also be made upon conditions like the presentation of an interim certificate of completion being met. Ensure any new letter of credit is in place before amending the contract.

If security deposits exceed the necessary amount due to contract price changes, the excess must be returned to the contractor. Similarly, you should reduce the face amount of the letter of credit to align with the decreased risk, following the procedure outlined for increasing the amount.

Difficulty fulfilling contract requirements

If a contractor shows signs of difficulty fulfilling the contract requirements and there is financial security in place, you should immediately notify the surety company. This step ensures proactive management of risks associated with contract fulfillment.

Custody and safekeeping of financial security and handling of bills of exchange

Bills of exchange used as security deposits for offers must be uncashed and securely stored until a successful offer is chosen, or for up to 1 year, whichever occurs first. If a contract has not been awarded by the year's end, the bill must be exchanged for one with a current date.

Bills of exchange and accompanying offer documentation are sent by the offer receiving unit to the Finance Sector of PSPC for secure holding. The Finance Sector confirms receipt in writing to the offer receiving unit. The receiving unit provides appropriate documentation to the buyer for the procurement file.

Bills of exchange after offer acceptance

Once an offer is accepted the bill of exchange is required as security until completion of the contract. If requested by the contractor, the bill of exchange should be retained uncashed and stored in secure approved facilities. If suitable storage is unavailable, the bill of exchange must be sent to the directorate responsible for financial operations for safekeeping.

If no such request is made, the bill should be deposited directly into the Consolidated Revenue Fund (CRF) in accordance with public money handling regulations.

Returning bills of exchange

Bills of exchange must be returned to the contractor if the offer is rejected or accepted. The bill is not needed as ongoing security.

Managing received securities

All bills of exchange received as part of contract security must be forwarded for deposit to the directorate responsible for financial operations in the CRF . This is in line with the Receipt and Deposit of Public Money Regulations .

Security for plans and specifications

Security deposits provided for the return of plans and specifications are forfeited if they are not returned in a timely and satisfactory condition. You must immediately inform the relevant manager in the directorate responsible for financial operations if a security deposit is forfeited.

Recording and safekeeping

The Finance Sector of PSPC is responsible for recording all received government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, and irrevocable standby letters of credit in the accounting records of PSPC and the Accounts of Canada. Directorates must notify the Finance Sector of all such receipts regardless of where they are held.

Safekeeping methods for negotiable instruments

Safekeeping of government bonds, negotiable instruments and letters of credit can be ensured through:

custody by the directorate for financial operations

secure storage by the directorate using approved security equipment, as per Government Contracts Regulations

use of the Security Deposit Division facilities

Additional security measures

You must consider the following additional security measures:

the adequacy of departmental security equipment should be assessed regularly with guidance available through the PSPC Security Equipment Catalogue

in the absence of proper security equipment, use a designated deposit form to send all deposits to the directorate responsible for financial operations

to minimize risk and ensure security, transmit bonds directly to the directorate from where the security is first received

registered and hypothecated bonds must be sent via registered mail

bearer bonds may be sent using secure transport methods

additional insurance should be considered for bearer bonds sent through the “money packet” system to cover values exceeding Canada Post’s standard indemnity limit of $100

the directorate responsible for financial operations also manages the clipping of coupons from bearer bonds and coordinates their redemption
